elements.sent-2411-001: We present an extensive study of quadrupole-hexadecapole correlation effects in even-even Sm and Gd isotopes with neutron number $N=88-106$.
elements.sent-2411-002: The calculations are performed in the framework of the Gogny energy density functional (EDF) with the D1S parametrization and the $sdg$ interacting boson model (IBM).
elements.sent-2411-003: The quadrupole-hexadecapole constrained self-consistent mean-field potential energy surface is mapped onto the expectation value of the $sdg$-boson Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-004: This procedure determines the parameters of the $sdg$-IBM Hamiltonian microscopically.
elements.sent-2411-005: Calculated excitation energies and transition strengths are compared to the ones obtained with a simpler $sd$-IBM, as well as with the experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-006: The Gogny-EDF mapped $sdg$-IBM reproduces spectroscopic properties of the studied nuclei as reasonably as in the case of the previous $sdg$-boson mapping calculations that were based on the relativistic EDF, indicating that the axial quadrupole-hexadecapole method is sound regardless of whether relativistic or nonrelativistic EDF is employed.
elements.sent-2411-007: The mapped $sdg$-IBM improves some of the results in lighter Sm and Gd isotopes compared to the mapped $sd$-IBM, implying the existence of significant hexadecapole correlations in those nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-008: For those nuclei with $N \geq 94$, hexadecapole effects are minor, and the only significant difference between the two boson models can be found in the description of $E0$ monopole transitions.
elements.sent-2411-009: The importance of studying deformations in nuclei and their effects on various nuclear properties, e.g. excitation energies and transition strengths, has been recognized for decades \cite{BM,RS .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The most commonly present and extensively studied nuclear deformation is of quadrupole type, and their effects on the positive-parity states are well known.
elements.sent-2411-011: The higher-order correlations which affect the positive-parity states, the hexadecapole correlations, have been significantly less studied, due to their effects often being overshadowed by large quadrupole correlations.
elements.sent-2411-012: Nevertheless, the presence of hexadecapole correlations has been found in a wide range of nuclei \cite{gupta2020, ryssens2023 , and the main effect of such correlations is the appearance of the $4^+$ band in the low-lying excitation energy spectra of some even-even nuclei, with an enhanced $B(E4)$ transition strength from the $4^+$ band head state to the $0^+$ ground state.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Hexadecapole correlations have also been found to be important in describing certain nuclear reactions and decays.
elements.sent-2411-014: The interacting boson model (IBM) \cite{IBM is one of the most commonly employed models for studying how nuclear deformations affect low-lying spectra \cite{casten1988 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The main assumption of the model is that a nucleus can be approximated as a system consisting of a core, represented by the closest doubly-magic nucleus, and the valence nucleons, which form collectives pairs (or bosons).
elements.sent-2411-016: Interactions among valence bosons give rise to the low-lying spectra and transitions.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the simplest version of the model, the $sd$-IBM-1, the nucleons primarily couple into $s$ $(J^{\pi =0^+)$ and $d$ $(J^{\pi =2^+)$ bosons, and the neutron and proton bosons are assumed to be identical \cite{IBM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: One particular extension of the simple $sd$-IBM is the inclusion of $g$ bosons $(J^{\pi =4^+)$, allowing for the study of hexadecapole collectivity in nuclei.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The importance of $g$ bosons in describing low-lying collective states has been extensively studied \cite{casten1988, otsuka1981, otsuka1982, otsuka1985, otsuka1988, devi-kota1990, kuyucak1994, vanisacker2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In recent years, a method has been developed, which is to derive the parameters of the IBM Hamiltonian from self-consistent mean-field (SCMF) calculations based on a microscopic energy density functional (EDF) \cite{nomura2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The method provides low-energy collective energy spectra and reduced transition probabilities, and has been successfully applied to study quadrupole \cite{nomura2008, nomura2010, nomura2011pt, nomura2012tri , octupole \cite{nomura2013oct, nomura2014 , and, recently, hexadecapole correlations \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we present an extension of Refs.~\cite{lotina2023,lotina2024 by studying the low-energy quadrupole and hexadecapole collective states of even-even rare-earth isotopes $^{150-168}$Sm and $^{152-170}$Gd, ranging from transitional to highly deformed nuclei, based on the Gogny force \cite{GOGNY1975 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The present calculation employs parametrization D1S \cite{BERGER1984 of the Gogny interaction, which has already been extensively employed \cite{Robledo2019,hilaire2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The combination of the Gogny EDFs with the IBM through the mapping procedure has been considered and shown to be valid to describe a number of nuclear structure phenomena, including coexistence of quadrupole shapes (e.g., Refs.~\cite{nomura2016zr,nomura2017ge ) and octupole correlations (e.g., Refs.~\cite{nomura2015,nomura2021oct-u,nomura2021oct-ba,nomura2021oct-zn ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Recently, Gogny Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov (HFB) calculations were performed to compute potential energy surfaces (PESs) have been computed with constraints on the quadrupole and hexadecapole deformation parameters \cite{kumar-robledo2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this study, we carry out the mapping procedure by using the Gogny-D1S PESs in order to examine the effects of the quadrupole-hexadecapole coupling on the low-lying spectra of the considered nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-027: Since in previous studies \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 , calculations were performed within the framework of the constrained relativistic mean field (MDC-RMF) model \cite{lu2014, zhao2017 , with the relativistic density-dependent point-coupling (DD-PC1) functional \cite{DDPC1, niksic2011 , this work will allow us to study how the quadrupole-hexadecapole mapping method depends on the choice of the EDF.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: On the other hand, we analyze positive parity bands in heavy Sm and Gd isotopes as well as the effects of hexadecapole collectivity in heavy neutron-rich nuclei, far from the valley of stability.
elements.sent-2411-029: The results of the calculations are compared with the ones obtained with a simpler $sd$-IBM, as well as with the available experimental data \cite{data .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: The theoretical framework, i.e., the Gogny-D1S HFB approach and the $sd$-IBM and $sdg$-IBM, is outlined in Sec.~\ref{sec:model .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Potential energy surfaces computed with both SCMF and IBM calculations are presented in Section~\ref{sec:pes .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Spectroscopic properties, including the excitation spectra of low-lying states, electric quadrupole, hexadecapole, and monopole transition properties, are discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Section~\ref{sec:summary is devoted to the concluding remarks and work perspectives.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In order to obtain the SCMF PES for the studied nuclei, the HFB equations have been solved with constraints on the axial quadrupole $\hat{Q _{20}$ and hexadecapole $\hat{Q _{40}$ moment operators \cite{kumar-robledo2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The axial quadrupole $\beta_{20 \equiv \beta_2$ and hexadecapole $\beta_{40 \equiv \beta_4$ deformation parameters are related to the expectation values of the multipole moment operators as \cite{kumar-robledo2023 : \begin{equation}\beta_{\lambda} = \frac{\sqrt{4 \pi (2 \lambda + 1)}}{3 R^{\lambda} A} \left\langle \hat{Q}_{\lambda 0} \right\rangle, \label{eq1}\end{equation} where $\lambda=2$ or 4, $R=1.2 A^{1/3}$ fm and $A$ is the nucleon number.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Details on the Gogny-D1S HFB calculations can be found in Refs.
elements.sent-2411-038: In order to calculate low-lying excitation energies and transition strengths, we have employed the $sdg$-IBM-1 model, referred to simply as $sdg$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-039: The Hamiltonian is built in the same way as in Ref. \cite{lotina2024 : \begin{equation}\hat{H} = \epsilon_d \hat{n}_d + \epsilon_g \hat{n}_g + \kappa \hat{Q}^{(2)} \cdot \hat{Q}^{(2)} + \kappa(1- \chi^2) \hat{Q}^{(4)} \cdot \hat{Q}^{(4)}, \label{eq2}\end{equation} with $\hat{n _d= d^{\dagger \cdot \tilde{d}$ and $\hat{n _g= g^{\dagger \cdot \tilde{g}$ being the $d$- and $g$-boson number operators, respectively, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{Q}^{(2)} = (s^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d} + d^{\dagger} \times s) + \chi \Big [ \frac{11 \sqrt{10}}{28}(d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d})^{(2)} - \frac{9}{7} \sigma (d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{g} + g^{\dagger} \times \tilde{g})^{(2)} + \frac{3 \sqrt{55}}{14} (g^{\dagger} \times \tilde{g})^{(2)} \Big ] \end{aligned} \label{eq3}\end{equation} being the quadrupole operator, and \begin{equation}\hat{Q}^{(4)} = s^{\dagger} \times \tilde{g} + g^{\dagger} \times s \label{eq4}\end{equation} being the hexadecapole operator.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: This Hamiltonian represents a modified version of the one used in Ref. \cite{vanisacker2010 , which satisfies the conditions of three symmetry limits: $[\textnormal{U(6) \supset \textnormal{U(5) ] \otimes \textnormal{U(9)}$, SU(3) and O(15) \cite{kota1987 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Here the quadrupole-quadrupole boson interaction is taken to be attractive, $\kappa<0$, and a single $g$ boson level is, to a good approximation, above that of a $d$ boson, $\epsilon_d < \epsilon_g$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition, the requirement of the SU(3) symmetry limit leads to the constraints that $-1 \leq \chi \leq +1$ and $-1 \leq \chi \sigma \leq +1$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The parameters $\epsilon_d$, $\epsilon_g$, $\kappa$, $\chi$ and $\sigma$ are determined by the mapping procedure \cite{nomura2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The $sdg$-IBM is connected to the geometric model through a coherent state \cite{ginocchio1980 , $\ket{\phi \sim (1+ \tilde{\beta _2 d^{\dagger _0 + \tilde{\beta _4 g^{\dagger _0)^{N_B \ket{0}$, with $N_B$ representing the number of bosons, determined by the nearest doubly-magic nucleus, and $\ket{0}$ representing the boson vacuum.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Note that $\tilde{\beta _2$ and $\tilde{\beta _4$ represent the axial bosonic quadrupole and hexadecapole deformation parameters, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: For Gd and Sm isotopes with $N<106$, the core is represented by the doubly magic nucleus $^{132}$Sn.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the case of $^{168}$Sm and $^{170}$Gd, the corresponding core is $^{176}$Sn since the neutrons occupy the upper half of the neutron major shell $N=82-126$.
elements.sent-2411-048: The parameters of the $sdg$-IBM are fitted so that the PES of the IBM, $E_{\textnormal{IBM (\tilde{\beta _2, \tilde{\beta _4)= \bra{\phi \hat{H \ket{\phi /\braket{\phi|\phi}$, should be approximately equal to the Gogny-HFB PES in the vicinity of the minimum: \begin{equation}E_{\textnormal{SCMF}}(\beta_2, \beta_4) \approx E_{\textnormal{IBM}}(\tilde{\beta}_2, \tilde{\beta}_4) \; . \label{eq5}\end{equation} The relation between axial bosonic and fermionic parameters is assumed to be linear, $\tilde{\beta _2 = C_2 \beta_2$, $\tilde{\beta _4 = C_4 \beta_4$ as in previous studies with the mapping procedure \cite{nomura2008, nomura2014, lotina2023, lotina2024 .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: This leaves us with 7 parameters which need to be fitted to reproduce the SCMF PES.
elements.sent-2411-050: For the $sd$-IBM calculations, we have employed the following Hamiltonian \cite{IBM : \begin{equation}\hat{H}_{sd} = \epsilon_d \hat{n}_d + \kappa \hat{Q}^{(2)} \cdot \hat{Q}^{(2)}, \label{eq6}\end{equation} where the $sd$-IBM quadrupole operator reads: \begin{equation}\hat{Q}^{(2)} = s^{\dagger} \tilde{d} + d^{\dagger} s + \chi \left ( d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d} \right )^{(2)}. \label{eq7}\end{equation} The parameters $\epsilon_d$, $\kappa$, $\chi$ in the $sd$-IBM Hamiltonian are fixed by a 1D mapping of the Gogny-D1S PES along the $\beta_4=0$ line: \begin{equation}E_{\textnormal{SCMF}}(\beta_2, \beta_4=0)= E_{sd-\textnormal{IBM}}(\tilde{\beta}_2), \label{eq8}\end{equation} and the relation between the bosonic and fermionic deformation parameters is assumed to be linear $\tilde{\beta _2 = C^{sd _2 \beta_2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In order to calculate transition strengths with both the $sdg$-IBM and $sd$-IBM, the transition operators are constructed in the same way as in Refs.. \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The quadrupole transition operator is of the form: \begin{equation}\hat{T}(E2)_{sdg/sd} = e_2^{sdg/sd} \hat{Q}^{(2)}_{sdg/sd}, \label{eq9}\end{equation} with $\hat{Q ^{(2) _{sdg/sd}$ representing the quadrupole operator of the $sdg$ (\ref{eq3 ) or $sd$ (\ref{eq7 ) boson model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The hexadecapole operator is chosen, in the case of $sdg$-IBM, to be: \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E4)}_{sdg} = e_4^{sdg} \left [ s^{\dagger} \tilde{g} + g^{\dagger} s + (d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d})^{(4)} \right ], \label{eq10}\end{equation} while, for $sd$-IBM, it is constructed as: \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E4)}_{sd} = e_4^{sd} (d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d})^{(4)}. \label{eq11}\end{equation} The quadrupole and hexadecapole effective charges $e_2^{sdg/sd , e_4^{sdg/sd}$ are fitted in order to reproduce the measured transition strengths $B(E2; 2_1^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ and $B(E4; 4_1^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$, from the first $2^+$ and $4^+$ states to the ground state $0^+$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The monopole transition operator \cite{vanisacker2012 reads \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E0)}_{sdg} = (e_n N + e_p Z) \left ( \eta \frac{\hat{n}_d}{N_B} + \gamma \frac{\hat{n}_g}{N_B} \right ), \label{eq12}\end{equation} for the $sdg$-IBM, and \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E0)}_{sd} = (e_n N + e_p Z) \eta \frac{\hat{n}_d}{N_B},\end{equation} for the $sd$-IBM.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Following Ref. \cite{vanisacker2012 , we set neutron and proton effective charges to the values $e_p=e$ and $e_n=0.5e$, while the values of parameters $\eta$ and $\gamma$ are taken to be $\eta=\gamma=0.75$ fm$^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Experimental data for energies and transition strengths have been taken from the NNDC database \cite{data .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =\textwidth]{Sm_Gogny.png} \caption{Axially-symmetric quadrupole ($\beta_{20}$) and hexadecapole ($\beta_{40}$) constrained potential energy surfaces for $^{150-168}$Sm. The energy difference between neighbouring contours is 0.1 MeV. The absolute minimum is indicated by an open triangle. Results have been obtained with the Gogny-D1S energy density functional.} \label{SMGOGNY} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{Gd_Gogny.png} \caption{The same as in Fig. \ref{SMGOGNY}, but for $^{152-170}$Gd.} \label{GDGOGNY} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =\textwidth]{Sm_IBM.png} \caption{The same as in Fig. \ref{SMGOGNY}, but for the mapped $sdg$-IBM potential energy surfaces of $^{150-168}$Sm.} \label{SMIBM} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =\textwidth]{Gd_IBM.png} \caption{The same as in Fig. \ref{SMGOGNY}, but for the mapped $sdg$-IBM potential energy surfaces of $^{152-170}$Gd.} \label{GDIBM} \end{center}
elements.section-2411-003: Mapping the SCMF results onto the IBM space\label{sec:pes}
elements.sent-2411-057: Figures \ref{SMGOGNY and \ref{GDGOGNY show the Gogny-D1S PESs obtained for even-even Sm and Gd isotopes with neutron numbers within the range $N=88-106$, up to an energy of 2.7 MeV above the HFB ground state.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The PESs for the $N=106$ isotones are not shown due to their similarity to those of the $N=104$ ones.
elements.sent-2411-059: As can be seen from the figures, the quadrupole and hexadecapole deformations associated with the absolute minima of the PESs start from the values $\beta_2^{\textnormal{min =0.2$ and $\beta_{4 ^{\textnormal{min =0.08$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: They increase with neutron number until $N=94$, after which hexadecapole deformations start decreasing, dropping to $\beta_{4 ^{\textnormal{min =0.04$ at $N=106$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Similar to previous (mapped) IBM calculations, based on the relativistic DD-PC1 functional \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 , Sm isotopes exhibit larger HFB ground state $\beta_4$ values as compared to Gd isotopes.}
elements.sent-2411-062: On the other hand, the ground state $\beta_2$ deformations increase up to $N=94$.
elements.sent-2411-063: For larger neutron numbers, those quadrupole deformations decrease, reaching the value $\beta_{2 ^{\textnormal{min =0.32$ for $N=106$ isotopes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: An important thing to observe is that the PESs appear to be "tilted" with respect to $\beta_{20}$ and $\beta_{40}$ axes, indicating a strong coupling between the axial quadrupole and hexadecapole degrees of freedom, which has already been discussed in Ref. \cite{kumar-robledo2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Note, that the results obtained for $N=88-96$ isotopes agree well with the ones obtained with the DD-PC1 EDF \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The corresponding (mapped) $sdg$-IBM PESs are depicted in Figs.~\ref{SMIBM and \ref{GDIBM .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The mapping procedure reproduces basic features of the fermionic PESs such as the position of the absolute minima, the oblate saddle points for $N=88$ nuclei as well as the overall shape of the surface.
elements.sent-2411-068: The strong quadrupole-hexadecapole coupling is well reproduced in $N \leq 100$ isotopes, while in $N > 100$ isotopes, the coupling is predicted to be less strong.
elements.sent-2411-069: Note that the IBM PESs are significantly flatter than the HFB ones, due to the restricted boson (valence) space as compared to the Gogny-HFB model space.
elements.sent-2411-070: This also explains why the mapping procedure is not able to reproduce the ``elongated'' shapes of the PESs for $N \geq 100$ nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-071: The 1D $sd$-IBM mapping has also been carried out in all the studied isotopes to reproduce some of the properties of the $E_{\textnormal{SCMF (\beta_2, \beta_4=0)$ curve such as the position of the absolute minimum, the position and energy of the oblate saddle point, and the energy of the spherical $\beta_2=0$ configuration.}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \linewidth]{sdg_parameters.png} \caption{Parameters of the $sdg$ - IBM Hamiltonian Eq.(\ref{eq2}), as functions of the neutron number $N$.} \label{sdgpar} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =0.6\linewidth]{sd_parameters.png} \caption{Parameters of the $sd$ - IBM Hamiltonian Eq.(\ref{eq6}), as functions of the neutron number $N$.} \label{sdpar} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-072: The $sdg$- and $sd$-IBM parameters are shown in Figs.
elements.sent-2411-073: The $d$-boson energy $\epsilon_d$ has a very similar behaviour in both models.
elements.sent-2411-074: It decreases up to $N=100$, after which it stops decreasing, and even increases at $N=106$, which corresponds to lower $\beta_2^{\rm min}$ values.
elements.sent-2411-075: Moreover, the parameters $\kappa$, $\chi$ and $C_2$ also display a similar behaviour in both models, whereas in the $sdg$-IBM the $\chi$ parameter is characterized by significantly lower absolute values, compared to the $sd$-IBM ones.
elements.sent-2411-076: Regarding those parameters appearing only in the $sdg$-IBM, the $g$-boson energy $\epsilon_g$ takes values between $\epsilon_g=1.1$ and $\epsilon_g=1.3$ MeV.
elements.sent-2411-077: The $C_4$ parameter decreases until $N=96$, after which it starts to increase, which corresponds to $\beta_4^{\rm min}$ values becoming lower for $N \geq 96$ isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, with increasing neutron number, the parameter $\sigma$, which accounts for the quadrupole-hexadecapole coupling in the $sdg$-IBM, drops from $\sigma=2.8$ to $\sigma=1.0$, which also corresponds to a decrease in $\beta_4^{\textnormal{min }$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: This explains why the coupling is predicted to be less strong in $N > 100$ isotopes by the $sdg$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-080: It should also be noted that $\sigma$ decreases faster in Gd isotopes, compared to the Sm ones, which can be explained from the fact that $\beta_4^{\textnormal{min }$ values are smaller in Gd isotopes with the same $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In this section, we discuss the excitation energies and transition strengths obtained from the diagonalization of the IBM Hamiltonian, using the computer program ARBMODEL \cite{arbmodel .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The results of the $sdg$-IBM and $sd$-IBM will be compared to illustrate the effects of $g$ bosons.
elements.sent-2411-083: The results obtained from both models will also be compared with the available experimental data at NNDC \cite{data .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =\linewidth]{ground_state_band.png} \caption{Excitation energies of the yrast band states up to spin $J^{\pi}=14^+$ as functions of the neutron number $N$ within the mapped $sdg$-IBM (left column) and $sd$-IBM (right column), represented by solid symbols connected by solid lines. Experimental data are taken from Ref.~\cite{data}, and are depicted as open symbols connected by dotted lines.} \label{yrast} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-084: Figure \ref{yrast shows the excitation energies corresponding to the yrast band states with even spin $J^{\pi =2^+$ to $14^+$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: As expected, the $sdg$-IBM improves the description of the excitation energies of higher-spin states with $J^{\pi = 12^+$ and $14^+$ in transitional $N=88$ nuclei.}
elements.sent-2411-086: For Sm nuclei, the $sdg$-IBM gives a better description of the energy levels up to $N=92$ than the $sd$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-087: This can be explained by the fact that large amounts of $g$-boson contributions are present in the higher-spin states, as indeed, the expectation value of the $g$-boson number operator $\braket{\hat{n _g}$ is greater or equal to 1 in those isotopes with $N\leq 92$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Note that for Sm isotopes with $N \leq 92$, the $\beta_4^{\rm min}$ values are larger compared to those for Gd isotopes with the same $N$.
elements.sent-2411-089: For the $N > 92$ isotopes, there is no significant difference between both models concerning the description of the ground state bands.
elements.sent-2411-090: This is not surprising, since as the number of valence nucleons increases for deformed nuclei even the $sd$-IBM is good enough to provide an accurate description of the yrast-band levels.
elements.sent-2411-091: In any case, in general the $sdg$-IBM does provide some significant improvements in the description of the yrast band, compared to the simpler $sd$-IBM.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{0+.png} \caption{The same as in Fig.~\ref{yrast}, but for the excitation energies of $0_{2}^+$, $2_{3}^+$, and $4_{3}^+$ states.} \label{0+} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-092: Figure~\ref{0+ depicts the excitation energies of the $0_{2 ^+$, $2_{3 ^+$ and $4_{3 ^+$ states, which are associated with the $K^{\pi =0^+$ band in the present study.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The $sdg$-IBM predicts a significantly low-lying $4_3^+$ level for $N=88$ isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-094: This agrees better with the experimental data than in the corresponding $sd$-IBM results.
elements.sent-2411-095: For other isotopes, there is no significant difference between the two models, as they both predict similar energies for all three states.
elements.sent-2411-096: Overall, the $sdg$-IBM does not improve the description of the $0^+$ band significantly.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gamma_vibrational_band.png} \caption{The same as in Fig.~\ref{yrast}, but for the excitation energies of $2_{2}^+$, $3_{1}^+$, and $4_{2}^+$ states.} \label{gamma} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-097: Figure \ref{gamma shows the excitation energies of the $2^+_2$, $3^+_1$, and $4^+_2$ states, which can be identified as members of the $\gamma$-vibrational band in the present calculation.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Both models predict similar energies for Sm and Gd isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-099: Those energies mostly agree well with the experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-100: The description of the $\gamma$-vibrational band could be improved by including both three-body terms \cite{nomura2012tri and the $\gamma$ degree of freedom at the HFB level.}
elements.sent-2411-101: However, such an extension is beyond the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-102: Work along these lines will be reported in future publications.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{E2.png} \caption{$B(E2)$ transition strengths in the ground state band of the well-deformed $N=90$ (first row) and $N=92$ (second row) isotopes as functions of spin $J$, computed with the mapped $sdg$-IBM (solid curves) and $sd$-IBM (dotted curves). The experimental data, represented by solid circles, are taken from Ref.~\cite{data}.} \label{E2} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-103: Figure \ref{E2 shows the $B(E2; J \rightarrow J-2)$ transition strengths in the ground state bands of the well deformed $N=90$ and 92 isotones.}
elements.sent-2411-104: These nuclei are specifically considered, since there are experimental data on $E2$ transitions.
elements.sent-2411-105: In Sm isotopes, the $sdg$-IBM significantly improves the description of $B(E2)$ transition strengths for $J^{\pi \geq 6^+$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: However, for $^{154}$Gd, the $sdg$-IBM predicts $B(E2)$ strengths significantly larger than the $sd$-IBM, which are however still smaller than the measured values.
elements.sent-2411-107: The discrepancy points towards some missing correlations other than the hexadecapole ones, that could also contribute to the $B(E2)$ strengths.
elements.sent-2411-108: Due to the large experimental error bars, in $^{156}$Gd it is not possible to conclude whether $sdg$-IBM improves the description of the $E2$ transitions in the yrast band or not.
elements.sent-2411-109: The fact that the $sdg$-IBM predicts larger $B(E2)$ transition strengths than the $sd$-IBM can be attributed to the large values of the parameter $\sigma$ (see Fig. \ref{sdgpar ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: In the case of $^{152, 154}$Sm and $^{154}$Gd the value $\sigma=2.8$ leads to a significant contribution to the $B(E2)$ transition strengths arising, from the $\left [ d^{\dagger \times \tilde{g + g^{\dagger \times \tilde{d \right ]^{(2)}$ part of the quadrupole $\hat{Q ^{(2)}$ operator of Eq.(\ref{eq3 ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Moreover, the $\sigma=2.1$ value obtained for $^{156}$Gd leads to similar $sdg$-IBM and $sd$-IBM $B(E2)$ strengths.
elements.sent-2411-112: Note that the $\sigma$ values for $^{154,156}$Gd are larger than those previously obtained \cite{lotina2024 using microscopic input from the relativistic DD-PC1 EDF.}

elements.sent-2411-115: Results for $B(E4; 4_1^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ strengths are plotted in panels (a) and (b) of the figure, while $B(E4)$ values from non-yrast $4_n^+$ (with $n=2,3,4$) to the $0^+$ ground states are shown in panels (c)-(h).
elements.sent-2411-116: The $4_2^+$ state typically belongs to the $\gamma$-vibrational band, where $g$ bosons do not play a significant role.
elements.sent-2411-117: Thus both models predict weak $E4$ transitions in this case.
elements.sent-2411-118: Large $sdg$-IBM $B(E4)$ values are obtained for the $4_3^+$ states in $^{150}$Sm and $^{152}$Gd.
elements.sent-2411-119: The strong $E4$ transition, predicted for the $4_4^+$ state in $^{154}$Gd, agrees well with the experimental identification of this state as a band head of the $K=4^+$ band \cite{data .}
elements.sent-2411-120: On the other hand, the $sd$-IBM gives negligibly small $B(E4)$ reduced transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-121: This indicates a significant difference between the $sdg$-IBM and the simpler $sd$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-122: Nevertheless, experimental data on $B(E4)$ strengths in rare-earth nuclei are still required to understand whether the $sdg$-IBM describes them better.

Experimental values, plotted as solid circles, are taken from Refs.~\cite{data,kibedi2005}.} \label{E0} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-123: Figure~\ref{E0 shows the monopole strengths $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_i^+ \rightarrow 0_j^+)$, with $i=2,3$ and $j=1,2$, as functions of the neutron number $N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: In $N \leq 92$ isotopes, both models yield similar values of monopole strengths.
elements.sent-2411-125: As can be seen from the figure, $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_2^+)$ vanishes for $N >92$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: The $sdg$-IBM predicts a much sharper decrease (increase) of $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ [$\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$], with increasing neutron number, than the $sd$-IBM.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: One can see from Figs.~\ref{E0 (a) and (c) that for those Sm and Gd nuclei with $N \geq 92$, the $sdg$-IBM predicts the monopole strengths $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ and $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ to be more or less similar to eath other, whereas in the $sd$-IBM these monopole transition strengths are quite at variance, that is, the $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ are larger than the $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ by a factor of 2 to 3.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: In addition, the $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ values obtained from the mapped $sdg$-IBM are smaller than those from the $sd$-IBM for neutron-rich nuclei with $N \geq 94$.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The reduction of the $0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+$ $E0$ transition in the case of the $sdg$-IBM has been already been discussed in Ref.~\cite{vanisacker2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-130: On the other hand, the $sdg$-IBM provides larger $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ values for the $N\geq 94$ nuclei than the $sd$-IBM.}
elements.sent-2411-131: These quantitative differences in the predicted $\rho^2(E0)$ values between the two sets of the IBM calculations represent the most significant difference between the $sdg$-IBM and $sd$-IBM in the neutron-rich Sm and Gd isotopes with $N \geq 94$.
elements.sent-2411-132: As in the case of the hexadecapole transitions, experimental information about the monopole transitions is limited.
elements.sent-2411-133: It is therefore not possible to tell whether the $sdg$-IBM represents an improvement over the simpler $sd$-IBM in this respect.
elements.sent-2411-134: We have presented an extensive analysis of the quadrupole-hexadecapole collectivity in even-even rare-earth isotopes $^{150-168}$Sm and $^{152-170}$Gd, and the effects of hexadecapole correlations on low-lying excitation energies and transition strengths.
elements.sent-2411-135: The calculations were performed in the framework of the Gogny D1S EDF and the $sdg$-IBM and $sd$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-136: The calculated results for the excitation spectra were shown to be in good agreement with the experimental data at the same level of accuracy as our previous mapped $sdg$-IBM studies based on the relativistic EDF \cite{lotina2023,lotina2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: We have thus found that the quadrupole-hexadecapole mapping method does not significantly depend on the choice of the EDF.
elements.sent-2411-138: The mapped $sdg$-IBM improves the description of the high-spin yrast states in the nuclei with $N=88$, as well as in the Sm isotopes with $N=90$ and 92.
elements.sent-2411-139: In heavier rare-earth nuclei, the contribution of $g$ bosons to the ground state band is insignificant.
elements.sent-2411-140: In the case of the excited $K=0^+$ and $\gamma$-vibrational bands, the $sdg$-IBM results did not differ significantly from the $sd$-IBM ones, with only some minor improvements of the $4_3^+$ states of the $K=0^+$ band at $N=88$ in the $sdg$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-141: In well-deformed $N=90$ and 92 nuclei, the $sdg$-IBM predicts stronger $B(E2)$ transition strengths for the yrast states with spin $J^{\pi \geq 6^+$, which is in good agreement with the experimental data.}
elements.sent-2411-142: The effect is more visible in $^{152,154}$Sm, for which pronounced hexadecapole mean-field minima were found in the PESs.
elements.sent-2411-143: The choice of the Gogny D1S EDF seems to lead to somewhat stronger yrast band $B(E2)$ values compared to the ones obtained with the relativistic DD-PC1 EDF.
elements.sent-2411-144: The existence of the $K=4^+$ band with an enhanced $B(E4; 4^+ \rightarrow 0^+)$ transition to the ground state is predicted by the $sdg$-IBM in $^{150}$Sm and $^{152,154}$Gd.
elements.sent-2411-145: In heavier isotopes, such bands are also predicted, however, the bandhead in those nuclei is predicted to be a higher-lying $4_{n \geq 5 ^+$ state.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Regarding monopole strengths, the $sdg$-IBM predicts lower values of $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_2^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ and larger values of $\rho^{2 (E0; 0_3^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ in neutron-rich isotopes with $N\geq 94$, than the $sd$-IBM.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: This is in good agreement with previous theoretical calculations, and represents the most pronounced hexadecapole correlation effect on the low-lying spectra in very neutron-rich Sm and Gd isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-148: Due to the lack of experimental data on such transitions, it remains to be seen whether the $sdg$-IBM correctly predicts the behaviour of monopole transitions in those isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-149: Now that the mapping method has been shown to be adequate for describing the quadrupole-hexadecapole collectivity in nuclei, regardless of the choice of the EDF, the method can be extended to include even-odd and odd-odd nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-150: The method should also be applied to heavier rare-earth nuclei up to W and Os nuclei, where more experimental data on $E4$ transitions are available, and to explore possible hexadecapole correlations in other regions of the nuclear chart such as actinides.
elements.sent-2411-151: The method could also be extended to include proton and neutron degrees of freedom (i.e., $sdg$-IBM-2), which become relevant to describe phenomena like mixed-symmetry states and scissor modes.
elements.sent-2411-152: This work has also been supported by the Spanish Agencia Estatal de Investigaci\'on (AEI) of the Ministry of Science and Innovation (MCIN) under grant agreement No. PID2021-127890NB-I00.. L.L.
elements.sent-2411-154: Rodr\'{\i guez-Guzm\'an was partially supported through the grant PID2022-136228NB-C22 funded by MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033/FEDER, UE and "ERDF A way of making Europe".}
elements.sent-2411-155: The most commonly present and extensively studied nuclear deformation is of quadrupole type, and its effects on the positive-parity states are well known.
elements.sent-2411-156: Nevertheless, the presence of hexadecapole correlations has been found in a wide range of nuclei \cite{gupta2020, ryssens2023 , and the main effect of such correlations is the appearance of the $4^+$ band in the low-lying excitation energy spectra of some even-even nuclei, with an enhanced $B(E4)$ transition strength from the $4^+$ bandhead state to the $0^+$ ground state.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Hexadecapole correlations have also been found to be important in describing certain nuclear reactions and decays \cite{ryssens2023, chi2023, engel2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Recently, Gogny Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov (HFB) calculations, with constraints set on the quadrupole and hexadecapole deformation parameters, were performed to compute potential energy surfaces (PESs) in rare-earth nuclei \cite{kumar-robledo2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Since, in previous studies \cite{lotina2023, lotina2024 , calculations were performed within the framework of the constrained relativistic mean field (MDC-RMF) model \cite{lu2014, zhao2017 , with the relativistic density-dependent point-coupling (DD-PC1) functional \cite{DDPC1, niksic2011 , this work will allow us to study how the quadrupole-hexadecapole mapping method depends on the choice of the EDF.}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: On the other hand, we analyze positive-parity bands in heavy Sm and Gd isotopes as well as the effects of hexadecapole collectivity in heavy neutron-rich nuclei, far from the valley of stability.
elements.sent-2411-161: Potential energy surfaces computed with both SCMF and IBM calculations are presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:pes .}
elements.sent-2411-162: The axial quadrupole $\beta_{20 \equiv \beta_2$ and hexadecapole $\beta_{40 \equiv \beta_4$ deformation parameters are related to the expectation values of the multipole moment operators as \cite{kumar-robledo2023 \begin{equation}\beta_{\lambda} = \frac{\sqrt{4 \pi (2 \lambda + 1)}}{3 R^{\lambda} A} \left\langle \hat{Q}_{\lambda 0} \right\rangle, \label{eq1}\end{equation} where $\lambda=2$ or 4, $R=1.2 A^{1/3}$ fm and $A$ is the nucleon number.}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: In order to calculate low-lying excitation energies and transition strengths, we have employed the $sdg$-IBM-1, referred to simply as $sdg$-IBM.
elements.sent-2411-164: The $sdg$-IBM is connected to the geometric model through a coherent state \cite{ginocchio1980 , $\ket{\phi \sim (1+ \tilde{\beta _2 d^{\dagger _0 + \tilde{\beta _4 g^{\dagger _0)^{N_B \ket{0}$, with $N_B$ representing the number of bosons, determined by the nearest doubly magic nucleus, and $\ket{0}$ representing the boson vacuum.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: For the $sd$-IBM calculations, we have employed the following Hamiltonian \cite{IBM : \begin{equation}\hat{H}_{sd} = \epsilon_d \hat{n}_d + \kappa \hat{Q}^{(2)} \cdot \hat{Q}^{(2)}, \label{eq6}\end{equation} where the $sd$-IBM quadrupole operator reads \begin{equation}\hat{Q}^{(2)} = s^{\dagger} \tilde{d} + d^{\dagger} s + \chi \left ( d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d} \right )^{(2)}. \label{eq7}\end{equation} The parameters $\epsilon_d$, $\kappa$, $\chi$ in the $sd$-IBM Hamiltonian are fixed by a 1D mapping of the Gogny-D1S PES along the $\beta_4=0$ line: \begin{equation}E_{\textnormal{SCMF}}(\beta_2, \beta_4=0)= E_{sd-\textnormal{IBM}}(\tilde{\beta}_2), \label{eq8}\end{equation} and the relation between the bosonic and fermionic deformation parameters is assumed to be linear $\tilde{\beta _2 = C^{sd _2 \beta_2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The hexadecapole operator is chosen, in the case of $sdg$-IBM, to be \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E4)}_{sdg} = e_4^{sdg} \left [ s^{\dagger} \tilde{g} + g^{\dagger} s + (d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d})^{(4)} \right ], \label{eq10}\end{equation} whereas for $sd$-IBM it is constructed as \begin{equation}\hat{T}^{(E4)}_{sd} = e_4^{sd} (d^{\dagger} \times \tilde{d})^{(4)}. \label{eq11}\end{equation} The quadrupole and hexadecapole effective charges $e_2^{sdg/sd}$ and $e_4^{sdg/sd}$ are fitted in order to reproduce the measured transition strengths $B(E2; 2_1^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$ and $B(E4; 4_1^+ \rightarrow 0_1^+)$, from the first $2^+$ and $4^+$ states to the ground state $0^+$, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width =\textwidth]{Sm_Gogny.png} \caption{Axially symmetric quadrupole ($\beta_{20}$) and hexadecapole ($\beta_{40}$) constrained potential energy surfaces for $^{150-166}$Sm. The energy difference between neighbouring contours is 0.1 MeV. The absolute minimum is indicated by an open triangle. Results have been obtained with the Gogny-D1S energy density functional.} \label{SMGOGNY} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-167: The strong quadrupole-hexadecapole coupling is well reproduced in $N \leq 100$ isotopes, whereas in $N > 100$ isotopes, the coupling is predicted to be less strong.
elements.sent-2411-168: The $d$-boson energy $\epsilon_d$ has a very similar behavior in both models.
elements.sent-2411-169: Moreover, the parameters $\kappa$, $\chi$ and $C_2$ also display a similar behavior in both models, whereas in the $sdg$-IBM the $\chi$ parameter is characterized by significantly lower absolute values, compared to the $sd$-IBM ones.
elements.sent-2411-170: This can be explained by the fact that large amounts of $g$-boson contributions are present in the higher-spin states, as indeed, the expectation value of the $g$-boson number operator $\braket{\hat{n _g}$ is greater than or equal to 1 in those isotopes with $N\leq 92$.}
elements.sent-2411-171: While the calculated energies of the $\gamma$-vibrational band states are in good agreement with the experimental data for the nuclei with $N \leq 90$ and $N \geq 96$, both models underestimate the energies for those with $N=92$ and 94.
elements.sent-2411-172: Overall, the presence of $g$ bosons in the IBM calculations does not seem to have any significant effects on the $\gamma$-vibrational band.
